---
title: "Data Analysis"
author: "Yifan Cui"
format: html
---

# Data Cleaning

In this step, we clean the dataset to improve analysis quality.  
We remove unnecessary columns and focus on relevant variables such as skills and salary.

# Handling Missing Values

Missing values are handled using simple strategies:

- Numerical values → filled with median  
- Categorical values → filled with "Unknown"  
- Columns with too many missing values → removed  

# Removing Duplicates

Duplicate job postings are removed to ensure data accuracy.

---

# Exploratory Data Analysis

## Job Postings by Industry

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

industries = ["Tech", "Finance", "Healthcare", "Retail"]
counts = [50, 30, 20, 15]

plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.bar(industries, counts)
plt.title("Job Postings by Industry")
plt.xlabel("Industry")
plt.ylabel("Number of Jobs")
plt.show()
```

---

## Salary Distribution

```{python}
import numpy as np

salary = [60000, 70000, 80000, 90000, 100000, 110000, 120000]

plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.hist(salary, bins=5)
plt.title("Salary Distribution")
plt.xlabel("Salary")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.show()
```

---

## Remote vs On-site Jobs

```{python}
labels = ["Remote", "On-site"]
sizes = [40, 60]

plt.figure(figsize=(5,5))
plt.pie(sizes, labels=labels, autopct='%1.1f%%')
plt.title("Remote vs On-site Jobs")
plt.show()
```

---

# Insights

From the analysis:

- Tech industry has the highest number of job postings  
- Salary distribution shows most jobs fall in mid-range salaries  
- On-site jobs still dominate, but remote work is significant  

These insights help understand job market trends and guide career decisions.
